qa_pairs.S: The light can come from different directions, which according to its incidence can be differentiated between: 'lateral', when it comes from the side, it is a light that highlights more the texture of the objects; 'frontal', when it comes from the front, it eliminates the shadows and the sensation of volume; 'zenithal', a vertical light of higher origin than the object, it produces a certain deformation of the figure; 'contrapicado', vertical light of lower origin, it deforms the figure in an exaggerated way; and 'backlight', when the origin is behind the object, thus darkening and diluting its silhouette.
qa_pairs.Q: What are the different types of light in painting?
qa_pairs.A: The different types of light in painting are: lateral, frontal, zenithal, contrapicado, and backlight.

qa_pairs.S: On the other hand, in relation to the shadow, the interrelation between light and shadow is called 'chiaroscuro'; if the dark area is larger than the illuminated one, it is called 'tenebrism'.
qa_pairs.Q: What is chiaroscuro and tenebrism in painting?
qa_pairs.A: Chiaroscuro is the interrelation between light and shadow in painting, while tenebrism is when the dark area is larger than the illuminated one.

qa_pairs.S: In exteriors, the main genre is landscape, perhaps the most relevant in relation to light in that its presence is fundamental, since any exterior is enveloped in a luminous atmosphere determined by the time of day and the weather and environmental conditions.
qa_pairs.Q: What is the main genre in exterior painting?
qa_pairs.A: Landscape

qa_pairs.S: Finally, the night has always been a singularity within painting, to the point of constituting a genre of its own: the nocturne.
qa_pairs.Q: What is the genre of painting that focuses on night scenes?
qa_pairs.A: Nocturne

qa_pairs.S: Leonardo was one of the first artists to be concerned with the degree of illumination of the painter's studio, suggesting that for nudes or carnations the studio should have uncovered lights and red walls, while for portraits the walls should be black and the light diffused by a canopy.
qa_pairs.Q: Who was concerned with the degree of illumination of the painter's studio?
qa_pairs.A: Leonardo da Vinci.

qa_pairs.S: . This tendency will have its climax in baroque tenebrism, in which darkness is used to add drama to the scene and to emphasize certain parts of the painting, often with a symbolic aspect.
qa_pairs.Q: What is tenebrism in painting?
qa_pairs.A: Tenebrism is a painting technique used in the Baroque period, in which darkness is used to add drama to the scene and to emphasize certain parts of the painting, often with a symbolic aspect.

qa_pairs.S: The Renaissance saw the emergence of the sfumato technique, traditionally attributed to Leonardo da Vinci, which consisted of the degradation of light tones to blur the contours and thus give a sense of remoteness.
qa_pairs.Q: What is the sfumato technique?
qa_pairs.A: The sfumato technique is the degradation of light tones to blur the contours and thus give a sense of remoteness. It is traditionally attributed to Leonardo da Vinci.

qa_pairs.S: Claudio de Lorena was one of the baroque painters who best knew how to represent light in his works, to which he gave a primordial importance at the time of conceiving the painting: the light composition served firstly as a plastic factor, being the basis with which he organized the composition, with which he created space and time, with which he articulated the figures, the architectures, the elements of nature;
qa_pairs.Q: Who was known for his ability to represent light in his baroque paintings?
qa_pairs.A: Claudio de Lorena.

qa_pairs.S: The 18th century was nicknamed the 'Age of Enlightenment', as it was the period in which the Enlightenment emerged, a philosophical movement that defended reason and science against religious dogmatism.
qa_pairs.Q: What was the nickname given to the 18th century period?
qa_pairs.A: The Age of Enlightenment.

qa_pairs.S: The first style of the century was Romanticism, a movement of profound renewal in all artistic genres, which paid special attention to the field of spirituality, fantasy, sentiment, love of nature, along with a darker element of irrationality, attraction to the occult, madness, dreams.
qa_pairs.Q: What was the first style of the 19th century?
qa_pairs.A: Romanticism

qa_pairs.S: Jean Auguste Dominique Ingres was a prolific author always faithful to classicism, to the point of being considered the champion of academic painting against 19th century romanticism.
qa_pairs.Q: Who was faithful to classicism and considered the champion of academic painting?
qa_pairs.A: Jean Auguste Dominique Ingres

qa_pairs.S: Romanticism and realism were the first artistic movements that rejected the official art of the time, the art taught in the academies — academicism
qa_pairs.Q: What style of art did Realism and Impressionism reject?
qa_pairs.A: Academicism

qa_pairs.S: In impressionist paintings the theme is light and its effects, beyond the anecdotal of places and characters.
qa_pairs.Q: What was the main subject of Impressionist paintings?
qa_pairs.A: Light and its effects

qa_pairs.S: The main representative of the Valencian school was Joaquín Sorolla, although the work of Ignacio Pinazo, Teodoro Andreu, Vicente Castell and Francisco Benítez Mellado is also noteworthy.
qa_pairs.Q: Who was the artist who was a master at capturing the light in nature and was the main representative of the Valencian school of luminism?
qa_pairs.A: Joaquín Sorolla was the main representative of the Valencian school of luminism.

qa_pairs.S: In abstract art, light loses the prominence it has in an image based on natural reality, but its presence is still perceived in the various tonal gradations and chiaroscuro games that appear in numerous works by abstract artists such as Mark Rothko, whose images of intense chromaticism have a luminosity that seems to radiate from the color of the work itself.
qa_pairs.Q: What role does light play in abstract art?
qa_pairs.A: Light loses prominence in abstract art but is still perceived in tonal gradations and chiaroscuro games.

qa_pairs.S: Post-painterly abstraction (also called 'New Abstraction') focused on geometrism, with an austere, cold and impersonal language, due to an anti-anthropocentric tendency that could be glimpsed in these years in art and culture in general, also present in pop-art, a style with which it coexisted.
qa_pairs.Q: What is the focus of post-painterly abstraction?
qa_pairs.A: Post-painterly abstraction focused on geometrism, with an austere, cold and impersonal language, due to an anti-anthropocentric tendency that could be glimpsed in these years in art and culture in general, also present in pop-art, a style with which it coexisted.

qa_pairs.S: Figuration returned again with hyperrealism — which emerged around 1965 — a trend characterized by its superlative and exaggerated vision of reality, which is captured with great accuracy in all its details, with an almost photographic aspect, in which Chuck Close, Richard Estes, Don Eddy, John Salt, and Ralph Goings stand out.
qa_pairs.Q: Which artists are associated with hyperrealism?
qa_pairs.A: Chuck Close, Richard Estes, Don Eddy, John Salt, and Ralph Goings.

qa_pairs.S: Leonardo was one of the first artists to be concerned with the degree of illumination of the painter's studio, suggesting that for nudes or carnations the studio should have uncovered lights and red walls, while for portraits the walls should be black and the light diffused by a canopy.
qa_pairs.Q: Were there any artists to be concerned with the degree of illumination of the painter's studio before Leonardo?
qa_pairs.A: No.

qa_pairs.S: The first style of the century was Romanticism, a movement of profound renewal in all artistic genres, which paid special attention to the field of spirituality, fantasy, sentiment, love of nature, along with a darker element of irrationality, attraction to the occult, madness, dreams.
qa_pairs.Q: What fields are paid attention to by Romanticism?
qa_pairs.A: field of spirituality, fantasy, sentiment, love of nature, along with a darker element of irrationality, attraction to the occult, madness, dreams.

qa_pairs.S: Jean Auguste Dominique Ingres was a prolific author always faithful to classicism, to the point of being considered the champion of academic painting against 19th century romanticism.
qa_pairs.Q: Who was faithful to classicism and considered the champion of academic painting?
qa_pairs.A: Jean Auguste Dominique Ingres

qa_pairs.S: Post-painterly abstraction (also called 'New Abstraction') focused on geometrism, with an austere, cold and impersonal language, due to an anti-anthropocentric tendency that could be glimpsed in these years in art and culture in general, also present in pop-art, a style with which it coexisted.
qa_pairs.Q: What is post-painterly abstraction also called?
qa_pairs.A: New Abstraction.

qa_pairs.S: Figuration returned again with hyperrealism — which emerged around 1965 — a trend characterized by its superlative and exaggerated vision of reality, which is captured with great accuracy in all its details, with an almost photographic aspect, in which Chuck Close, Richard Estes, Don Eddy, John Salt, and Ralph Goings stand out.
qa_pairs.Q: Was it the first time figuration returned with hyperrealism?
qa_pairs.A: No.

qa_pairs.S: 1 January – A visit by Thor the Walrus to Scarborough harbour, North Yorkshire overnight on New Year's Eve results in the town's New Year fireworks celebrations being cancelled to let the walrus rest for his journey to the Arctic. He was previously spotted at Pagham Harbour, Calshot, Hampshire in December 2022.
qa_pairs.Q: Why were the New Year fireworks celebrations cancelled in Scarborough?
qa_pairs.A: To let Thor the Walrus rest for his journey to the Arctic.

qa_pairs.S: 10 January. The UK government publishes the Strikes (Minimum Service Levels) Bill 2023, designed to require public sector organisations to provide a minimum service when their unions vote to strike.
qa_pairs.Q: What is the purpose of the Strikes (Minimum Service Levels) Bill 2023?
qa_pairs.A: To require public sector organisations to provide a minimum service when their unions vote to strike.

qa_pairs.S: Former Met Police officer David Carrick, one of the UK's most prolific sex offenders, is sentenced at Southwark Crown Court to 36 life sentences with a minimum term of 30 years in prison.
qa_pairs.Q: What is the sentence given to David Carrick?
qa_pairs.A: 36 life sentences with a minimum term of 30 years in prison.

qa_pairs.S: Greg Hands is named as the new Conservative Party chairman; Grant Shapps becomes the Secretary of State for Energy, Security and Net Zero in a newly-formed department; Kemi Badenoch is appointed as the first Secretary of State at the newly-created Department for Business and Trade, with continued responsibility as equalities minister.
qa_pairs.Q: Who are the new appointments in the UK government?
qa_pairs.A: Greg Hands as the new Conservative Party chairman, Grant Shapps as the Secretary of State for Energy, Security and Net Zero in a newly-formed department, and Kemi Badenoch as the first Secretary of State at the newly-created Department for Business and Trade, with continued responsibility as equalities minister.

qa_pairs.S: Lockdown Files: The latest leaked WhatsApp messages published by the Daily Telegraph are reported as appearing to show former Health Secretary Matt Hancock and Cabinet Secretary Simon Case joking about locking people in quarantine hotels.
qa_pairs.Q: What do the leaked WhatsApp messages show about Matt Hancock and Simon Case?
qa_pairs.A: The leaked WhatsApp messages show Matt Hancock and Simon Case joking about locking people in quarantine hotels.

qa_pairs.S: In England, the number of people testing positive for COVID-19 was estimated to be 1,298,600 (roughly 2.31% of the population around 1 in 45).
qa_pairs.Q: What is the estimated number of people testing positive for COVID-19 in England?

qa_pairs.S: A major incident is declared, with 35 injuries reported, after the 76m-long RV Petrel research vessel tips over at a dock in Leith..
qa_pairs.Q: What happened to the RV Petrel research vessel?
qa_pairs.A: The RV Petrel research vessel tipped over at a dock in Leith, with 35 injuries reported.

qa_pairs.S: Boris Johnson gives evidence to the cross-party Privileges Committee, relating to his conduct during Partygate. He insists that he 'did not lie' to the House of Commons and always made statements in good faith..
qa_pairs.Q: What did Boris Johnson say during his evidence to the Privileges Committee?
qa_pairs.A: Boris Johnson insisted that he did not lie to the House of Commons and always made statements in good faith.

qa_pairs.S: A report published by Diabetes UK indicates the UK is heading for what the charity describes as a 'rapidly escalating diabetes crisis', with 4.3 million people experiencing a form of diabetes, a further 850,000 estimated to be living with the disease but unaware of it, and another 2.4 million people at risk of developing the condition. Cases of diabetes are more prevalent in less affluent areas of the country..
qa_pairs.Q: How many people in the UK are estimated to be living with diabetes without being aware of it?

qa_pairs.S: Ford receives government approval for its 'BlueCruise' Level 2 autonomous driving technology..
qa_pairs.Q: What is the name of Ford's Level 2 autonomous driving technology that received government approval?
qa_pairs.A: 'BlueCruise'.

qa_pairs.S: Vodafone confirms it will begin switching off its 3G network from June, prompting concerns that people with older and more basic phones could experience 'digital poverty'.
qa_pairs.Q: What is Vodafone planning to do?
qa_pairs.A: Switch off its 3G network from June.

qa_pairs.S: The legal case Duke of Sussex v Mirror Group Newspapers begins at the High Court.
qa_pairs.Q: What legal case begins at the High Court?
qa_pairs.A: Duke of Sussex v Mirror Group Newspapers.

qa_pairs.S: Susan Cunliffe-Lister, Baroness Masham of Ilton, 87, British politician, member of the House of Lords (since 1970) and Paralympic champion (1960, 1964)..
qa_pairs.Q: What were Susan Cunliffe-Lister's achievements?
qa_pairs.A: Susan Cunliffe-Lister was a British politician, member of the House of Lords since 1970 and Paralympic champion in 1960 and 1964.

qa_pairs.S: Simon Emmerson, 67, English record producer, guitarist, DJ, musical director, founder of (Afro Celt Sound System)..
qa_pairs.Q: What was Simon Emmerson known for?
qa_pairs.A: Simon Emmerson was an English record producer, guitarist, DJ, musical director and founder of Afro Celt Sound System.

qa_pairs.S: 1 January – A visit by Thor the Walrus to Scarborough harbour, North Yorkshire overnight on New Year's Eve results in the town's New Year fireworks celebrations being cancelled to let the walrus rest for his journey to the Arctic.
qa_pairs.Q: Why were the New Year fireworks celebrations in Scarborough cancelled?
qa_pairs.A: To let the walrus rest for his journey to the Arctic.

qa_pairs.S: The government confirms it will not go ahead with a plan to privatise Channel 4.
qa_pairs.Q: What decision did the government make regarding the privatization of Channel 4?
qa_pairs.A: The government decided not to go ahead with the plan to privatize Channel 4.

qa_pairs.S: 12 January – Heavy rain and strong winds cause floods and travel disruption in parts of the UK, with over 60 flood warnings issued in England, 19 in Wales and 2 in Scotland.
qa_pairs.Q: What were the consequences of heavy rain and strong winds in the UK?
qa_pairs.A: Floods and travel disruption in parts of the UK.

qa_pairs.S: 26 January – Nicola Sturgeon confirms that Isla Bryson, a trans woman recently convicted of raping two women before her transition, has been moved from Cornton Vale women's prison to HMP Edinburgh men's prison, sparking debate about the Gender Recognition Reform (Scotland) Bill.
qa_pairs.Q: Why was Isla Bryson moved from Cornton Vale women's prison to HMP Edinburgh men's prison?
qa_pairs.A: Due to her recent conviction of raping two women before her transition.

qa_pairs.S: Ukraine's President Volodymyr Zelensky addresses a joint session of Parliament during his first visit to the UK since Russia invaded his country. He later visits Buckingham Palace for a meeting with the King.
qa_pairs.Q: What was the purpose of Ukraine's President Volodymyr Zelensky's visit to the UK?
qa_pairs.A: To address a joint session of Parliament.

qa_pairs.S: Data released by the Office for National Statistics indicates the UK narrowly avoided a recession at the end of 2022 following zero percent growth during October to December. This is also despite a fall in output of 0.5% during December due to strike action being staged prior to Christmas.
qa_pairs.Q: What does the data from the Office for National Statistics indicate?
qa_pairs.A: The UK narrowly avoided a recession at the end of 2022.

qa_pairs.S: Coronation of Charles III and Camilla. Buckingham Palace unveils the official Coronation logo, designed by Sir Jony Ive.
qa_pairs.Q: What was unveiled by Buckingham Palace for the Coronation of Charles III and Camilla?
qa_pairs.A: The official Coronation logo.

qa_pairs.S: COVID-19 in the UK: Data from the Office for National Statistics for the week ending 31 January indicates COVID-19 cases have risen in England for the first time in 2023, with 1.02 million cases, an increase of 8% from 941,800 the previous week. Data for Scotland and Wales is less clear.
qa_pairs.Q: What does the data from the Office for National Statistics indicate about COVID-19 cases in England?
qa_pairs.A: COVID-19 cases have risen in England for the first time in 2023.

qa_pairs.S: 11 February – The body of Brianna Ghey, a 16-year-old teenage transgender girl is found in Warrington Park in Cheshire, England.
qa_pairs.Q: Where was the body of Brianna Ghey found?
qa_pairs.A: In Warrington Park in Cheshire, England.

qa_pairs.S: 13 February – Former Metropolitan Police officer Wayne Couzens pleads guilty to three counts of indecent exposure during a hearing at the Old Bailey, including one committed four days before he killed Sarah Everard in 2021.
qa_pairs.Q: What did Wayne Couzens plead guilty to?
qa_pairs.A: Three counts of indecent exposure.

qa_pairs.S: 15 February. Inflation falls for the third month in a row, from 10.5% to 10.1%.
qa_pairs.Q: What happened to inflation in the UK?
qa_pairs.A: Inflation fell for the third month in a row.

qa_pairs.S: Nicola Sturgeon announces her resignation as First Minister of Scotland and Leader of the Scottish National Party after eight years in the role; she will stay on until her successor has been elected.
qa_pairs.Q: Who announced their resignation as First Minister of Scotland?
qa_pairs.A: Nicola Sturgeon.

qa_pairs.S: 16 February – The RMT announce four new days of train strikes for 16, 18 and 30 March, and 1 April.
qa_pairs.Q: What did the RMT announce?
qa_pairs.A: Four new days of train strikes.

qa_pairs.S: David Ballantyne Smith, a former security guard at the British embassy in Berlin who attempted to sell confidential information to the Russians, is sentenced to 13 years imprisonment following a trial at the Old Bailey.
qa_pairs.Q: What was David Ballantyne Smith sentenced to?
qa_pairs.A: 13 years imprisonment.

qa_pairs.S: Storm Otto strikes Scotland and parts of northern England, leaving around 30,000 homes without power and forcing a number of schools to close.
qa_pairs.Q: What was the impact of Storm Otto?
qa_pairs.A: Storm Otto left around 30,000 homes without power and forced the closure of a number of schools.

qa_pairs.S: COVID-19 in the UK: Office for National Statistics data for the week up to 7 February indicates that COVID-19 cases continued to increase in England, Wales and Scotland, but decreased in Northern Ireland.
qa_pairs.Q: Where did COVID-19 cases continue to increase?
qa_pairs.A: England, Wales, and Scotland.

qa_pairs.S: 18 February – Coronation of Charles III and Camilla: Twelve new pieces of music are commissioned by the King for his coronation, including a composition by Andrew Lloyd Webber.
qa_pairs.Q: How many new pieces of music were commissioned for the Coronation of Charles III and Camilla?

qa_pairs.S: 19 February – Police searching for Nicola Bulley, missing since 27 January, announce they have found a body in the River Wyre.
qa_pairs.Q: What did the police announce regarding the search for Nicola Bulley?
qa_pairs.A: They have found a body in the River Wyre during the search for Nicola Bulley.

qa_pairs.S: Wayne Couzens is sentenced to 19 months imprisonment after pleading guilty to three counts of indecent exposure in the months prior to the kidnap and murder of Sarah Everard.
qa_pairs.Q: What is Wayne Couzens sentenced for?
qa_pairs.A: Three counts of indecent exposure.

qa_pairs.S: A parole hearing for Charles Bronson, one of the UK's longest serving prisoners, is held at the Royal Courts of Justice. It is the second such hearing to be held in public.
qa_pairs.Q: Where is the parole hearing for Charles Bronson held?
qa_pairs.A: Royal Courts of Justice.

qa_pairs.S: Thomas Cashman, 34, is convicted of shooting dead nine-year-old Olivia Pratt-Korbel in her Liverpool home in August 2022.
qa_pairs.Q: Who was convicted of shooting Olivia Pratt-Korbel?
qa_pairs.A: Thomas Cashman.

qa_pairs.S: The Parliamentary Standards Committee recommends that former Scottish National Party MP Margaret Ferrier be suspended from the House of Commons for 30 days for breaching COVID-19 regulations in September 2020 when she took a train home from London following a positive COVID test.
qa_pairs.Q: Why does the Parliamentary Standards Committee recommend suspending Margaret Ferrier?
qa_pairs.A: For breaching COVID-19 regulations by taking a train home from London following a positive COVID test.

qa_pairs.S: Manchester becomes the first city in the UK to launch a tourist tax, with a £1-per room per night City Visitor Charge.
qa_pairs.Q: What is the City Visitor Charge in Manchester?
qa_pairs.A: £1-per room per night.

qa_pairs.S: Coronation of Charles III and Camilla: Organisers announce that among the changes to the ceremony for the coronation will be to invite people watching proceedings to swear allegiance to the King and his heirs.
qa_pairs.Q: What change will be made to the ceremony for the coronation of Charles III and Camilla?
qa_pairs.A: People watching proceedings will be invited to swear allegiance to the King and his heirs.

qa_pairs.S: The final UK rescue flight from the Sudanese capital of Khartoum takes off as the rescue of UK nationals comes to an end.
qa_pairs.Q: Where did the final UK rescue flight take off from?
qa_pairs.A: Khartoum.

qa_pairs.S: Eight people are stabbed, one fatally, in a street brawl near a nightclub in Bodmin, Cornwall.
qa_pairs.Q: How many people were stabbed in the street brawl in Bodmin?

qa_pairs.S: Alex Chalk, the Secretary of State for Justice, announces new rules for terrorists in prison in England and Wales which will see them limited to two boxes of books and prevented from leading religious meetings.
qa_pairs.Q: What are the new rules announced for terrorists in prison in England and Wales?
qa_pairs.A: The new rules limit terrorists to two boxes of books and prevent them from leading religious meetings.

qa_pairs.S: A huge wildfire covering 30 square miles (80 sq km), possibly the largest ever seen in the UK, is brought under control by the Scottish Fire and Rescue Service at Cannich in the Highlands.
qa_pairs.Q: Where was the wildfire brought under control?
qa_pairs.A: Cannich in the Highlands.

qa_pairs.S: House prices in the UK are reported to have fallen by 3.4% in the year to May, the biggest decline since July 2009. The average property price is now estimated at £260,736.
qa_pairs.Q: What was the percentage decline in house prices in the UK?

qa_pairs.S: A ban on e-scooters carried on Southeastern, Southern, Thameslink and Gatwick Express trains comes into force, due to concerns over fire risk.
qa_pairs.Q: Why was a ban on e-scooters carried on certain trains implemented?
qa_pairs.A: Due to concerns over fire risk.

qa_pairs.S: 2 June – A visibly emotional Phillip Schofield gives an interview with the BBC's Amol Rajan, in which he apologises and says his 'career is over' following the affair with a young male colleague.
qa_pairs.Q: Who did Phillip Schofield give an emotional interview to?
qa_pairs.A: BBC's Amol Rajan.

qa_pairs.S: Train strikes staged by the ASLEF union cause disruption to the 2023 FA Cup final and a Beyoncé Knowles concert.
qa_pairs.Q: What caused disruption to the 2023 FA Cup final and a Beyoncé Knowles concert?
qa_pairs.A: Train strikes staged by the ASLEF union.

qa_pairs.S: The Ministry of Defence confirms that UK Special Forces are at the centre of a war crimes investigation involving Afghanistan.
qa_pairs.Q: What is the subject of the war crimes investigation involving UK Special Forces?
qa_pairs.A: UK Special Forces.

qa_pairs.S: David Black, the chief executive of Ofwat, suggests that water bills are likely to rise in 2025 as water companies seek to cover the cost of improving services.
qa_pairs.Q: According to David Black, why are water bills likely to rise in 2025?
qa_pairs.A: To cover the cost of improving services.

qa_pairs.S: Two children die, while 15 other people are injured after a Land Rover hits a primary school in Wimbledon, south-west London. The crash is not treated as terror-related, but the driver is arrested on suspicion of causing death by dangerous driving.
qa_pairs.Q: What is the reason for the driver's arrest after a Land Rover hits a primary school in Wimbledon?
qa_pairs.A: Suspicion of causing death by dangerous driving.

qa_pairs.S: Wallasey pub shooting: Connor Chapman is found guilty of shooting dead 26-year-old Elle Edwards and injuring four others with a submachine gun. Co-defendant Thomas Waring is also found guilty of possessing a prohibited firearm and assisting an offender. The following day, Chapman is sentenced to a minimum of 48 years in prison, and Waring is given a nine-year prison term.
qa_pairs.Q: Who is found guilty of shooting dead Elle Edwards and injuring four others in the Wallasey pub shooting?
qa_pairs.A: Connor Chapman.

qa_pairs.S: Consumer finance expert Martin Lewis speaks to BBC Radio 4 about the growing use of deepfake AI technology, warning that more regulation is needed to prevent online scams.
qa_pairs.Q: What does Martin Lewis warn about regarding the use of deepfake AI technology?
qa_pairs.A: More regulation is needed to prevent online scams.

qa_pairs.S: Simon Emmerson, 67, English record producer, guitarist, DJ, musical director, founder of (Afro Celt Sound System).
qa_pairs.Q: What is Simon Emmerson known for?
qa_pairs.A: Being an English record producer, guitarist, DJ, musical director, and the founder of Afro Celt Sound System.

qa_pairs.S: In 1956, the increasingly pro-Soviet President Nasser of Egypt, announced the nationalization of the (French and British owned) Suez Canal, which was Egypt's main source of foreign currency.
qa_pairs.Q: What was the main source of foreign currency for Egypt that was nationalized by President Nasser?
qa_pairs.A: The Suez Canal.

qa_pairs.S: Israel was to attack Egypt, and Britain and France would then call on both sides to withdraw.
qa_pairs.Q: What was the plan of Britain, France, and Israel to seize the Suez Canal?
qa_pairs.A: Israel would attack Egypt, and Britain and France would then call on both sides to withdraw.

qa_pairs.S: Despite heavy casualties, the Palestinians claimed victory, while Fatah and the PLO (of which it formed part) became famous across the Arab world.
qa_pairs.Q: What was the outcome of the conflict between Palestinians and Israelis?
qa_pairs.A: The Palestinians claimed victory and Fatah and the PLO became famous across the Arab world.

qa_pairs.S: In early 1969 Levi Eshkol died in office of a heart attack and Golda Meir became Prime Minister with the largest percentage of the vote ever won by an Israeli party, winning 56 of the 120 seats after the 1969 election.
qa_pairs.Q: Who became the Prime Minister of Israel after Levi Eshkol's death?
qa_pairs.A: Golda Meir.

qa_pairs.S: In 1983 the May 17 Agreement was signed between Israel and Lebanon, paving the way for an Israeli withdrawal from Lebanese territory through a few stages.
qa_pairs.Q: What was the May 17 Agreement?
qa_pairs.A: The May 17 Agreement was signed between Israel and Lebanon, paving the way for an Israeli withdrawal from Lebanese territory through a few stages.

qa_pairs.S: Later that year, the Israeli electoral system was changed to allow for direct election of the prime minister.
qa_pairs.Q: What was changed in the Israeli electoral system?
qa_pairs.A: The Israeli electoral system was changed to allow for direct election of the prime minister.

qa_pairs.S: On 13 September 1993, Israel and the Palestine Liberation Organization (PLO) signed the Oslo Accords (a Declaration of Principles) on the South Lawn of the White House.
qa_pairs.Q: What were the Oslo Accords?

qa_pairs.S: Cornish entrance graves are part of an Early Bronze Age monument building tradition along the Atlantic Façade, where there are similar monuments in County Waterford, south-east Ireland (the Tramore tombs), and south-west Scotland (the Bargrennan cairns).

qa_pairs.S: A 2022 X-ray fluorescence and microwear analysis study of Bronze Age stone tools from Sennen, Lelant, and Truro found that the tools had been used to process semi-hard minerals, and detected traces of cassiterite tin ore on six implements, providing what the authors describe as 'the earliest secure evidence for tin exploitation in Britain'.
qa_pairs.Q: What is the significance of the X-ray fluorescence and microwear analysis study of Bronze Age stone tools from Sennen, Lelant, and Truro?
qa_pairs.A: It provides the earliest secure evidence for tin exploitation in Britain.

qa_pairs.S: A mould for a copper alloy racloir, a triangular blade with a central perforation, commonly found in France at the time but with only four examples of this type found in Britain, is found here, perhaps indicating that local metalsmiths were familiar with contemporary French metalwork forms, and may have been producing such work for export.
qa_pairs.Q: What is a racloir?
qa_pairs.A: A racloir is a triangular blade with a central perforation, commonly found in France at the time but with only four examples of this type found in Britain.

qa_pairs.S: The Trevose Head, Cataclews, and Harlyn Bay area in St Merryn parish comprises the largest collection of Early Bronze Age gold and metalwork in South West Britain.
qa_pairs.Q: Where is the largest collection of Early Bronze Age gold and metalwork in South West Britain located?
qa_pairs.A: In the Trevose Head, Cataclews, and Harlyn Bay area in St Merryn parish.

qa_pairs.S: This interrelation between body and spirit is inherent to Greek art, and when artists of later times imitated the Greek nude—as in the case of neoclassicism and academicism—stripped of this component, they produced lifeless works, focused on physical perfection, but without moral virtue.
qa_pairs.Q: What happens when artists imitate the Greek nude without the interrelation between body and spirit?
qa_pairs.A: They produce lifeless works, focused on physical perfection, but without moral virtue.

qa_pairs.S: The first exponent of the male nude is a type of figures representing athletes, gods or mythological heroes, called kouros (kouroi in plural), belonging to the Archaic period (7th century–5th century BC)—their female variant is the kore (korai in plural), which, however, they used to represent dressed.
qa_pairs.Q: What is the female variant of kouros?
qa_pairs.A: Kore (korai in plural).

qa_pairs.S: One of the first production centers of Hellenistic sculpture was Pergamon, whose workshop of sculptors from all over Greece established a style that, starting from a clear Lysipian influence, imprinted a dramatism to their figures that, primarily through the twisting of the body, expressed in an effective way the pain of the characters, as seen in the Dying Gaul of the Capitoline Museum (230 BC), the Ludovisi Gaul of the Museo delle Terme (230 BC), Menelaus supporting the body of Patroclus of the Loggia dei Lanzi (230–200 BC), also called Pasquino Group) or in the Marsyas of the Conservatory of Rome (230–200 BC).
qa_pairs.Q: What was the style of Hellenistic sculpture established by Pergamon?
qa_pairs.A: The stylewas characterized by dramatism and twisting of the body to express pain.

qa_pairs.S: The few representations of the nude in medieval art were limited to biblical passages that justified it, such as Adam and Eve in Paradise or the martyrdom and crucifixion of Jesus Christ.
qa_pairs.Q: What were the main biblical passages that justified the representation of the nude in medieval art?
qa_pairs.A: Adam and Eve in Paradise or the martyrdom and crucifixion of Jesus Christ.

qa_pairs.S: The Renaissance nude was inspired by classical Greco-Roman models, although with a different function from the one it had in antiquity: if in Greece the male nude exemplified the figure of the hero, in Renaissance Italy the nude has a more aesthetic character, more linked to a new way of understanding the world away from religious precepts, the human being again as the center of the universe.
qa_pairs.Q: What was the function of the Renaissance nude?
qa_pairs.A: The Renaissance nude had a more aesthetic character, more linked to a new way of understanding the world away from religious precepts, the human being again as the center of the universe.

qa_pairs.S: During the 15th century, certain forms inherited from Gothic art still survived in Italy, although they gradually gained in naturalism and veracity.
qa_pairs.Q: What was the influence of Gothic art on Italian art during the 15th century?
qa_pairs.A: Certain forms inherited from Gothic art still survived in Italy, although they gradually gained in naturalism and veracity.

qa_pairs.S: The culmination of the Renaissance nude occurred in the work of Michelangelo, for whom the naked human body had a divine character that gave it a dignity unmatched by any other contemporary nude.
qa_pairs.Q: Who is considered to have created the ultimate Renaissance nude?
qa_pairs.A: Michelangelo.

qa_pairs.S: The Venetians managed to harmonize the nude within more elaborate compositions, whether indoors or within the framework of a natural landscape, while their chromatic and lighting innovations gave greater realism and sensuality to the nude, with large and exuberant figures that began to move away from the classical canon
qa_pairs.Q: What was the contribution of the Venetian school to the nude?
qa_pairs.A: They managed to harmonize the nude within more elaborate compositions.

qa_pairs.S: During the Baroque period, the female nude continued to predominate as an object of pleasure for aristocratic patrons, who enjoyed this type of composition, where women generally played a subordinate role to men.
qa_pairs.Q: Who enjoyed the female nude during the Baroque period?
qa_pairs.A: Aristocratic patrons.

qa_pairs.S: Rubens attached great importance to the design of his figures, and for this he studied in depth the work of previous artists, from whom he took his best resources, especially—in what concerns the nude—from Michelangelo, Titian and Marcantonio Raimondi.
qa_pairs.Q: Which artists did Rubens study for his nude figures?
qa_pairs.A: Michelangelo, Titian and Marcantonio Raimondi.

qa_pairs.S: On the opposite side of Rubens' idealism is the work of Rembrandt, heir to the rounded forms of the Nordic nude of Gothic origin, with figures treated realistically, just as exuberant as those of Rubens, but more mundane, without hiding the folds of the flesh or the wrinkles of the skin, with a pathos that accentuates the raw materiality of the body, in its most humiliating and pitiful aspect.
qa_pairs.Q: How did Rembrandt treat the figures in his work?
qa_pairs.A: Rembrandt treated the figures in his work realistically, without hiding the folds of the flesh or the wrinkles of the skin, with a pathos that accentuates the raw materiality of the body, in its most humiliating and pitiful aspect.

qa_pairs.S: In the French field, Nicolas Poussin, an artist of a serene classicism, was perhaps the inaugurator of the academic nude, for being cultured and idealized, based on the representation in images of the erudite culture that had mythology and ancient history as its thematic base.
qa_pairs.Q: Who was perhaps the inaugurator of the academic nude in France?
qa_pairs.A: Nicolas Poussin.

qa_pairs.S: Among his works, in addition to the Resting Girl (portrait of Marie-Louise O'Murphy, mistress of Louis XV, and the youngest of five sisters all of whom Boucher painted), stand out: The Triumph of Venus (1740), Leda and the Swan (1742), Diana Resting after her Bath (1742), La toilette de Venus (1751), etc.
qa_pairs.Q: Who was the subject of Boucher's Resting Girl?
qa_pairs.A: Marie-Louise O'Murphy, mistress of Louis XV, and the youngest of five sisters all of whom Boucher painted.

qa_pairs.S: Between the end of the 18th century and the beginning of the 19th century, the foundations of contemporary society were laid, marked in the political field by the end of absolutism and the establishment of democratic governments—an impulse initiated with the French Revolution; and, in the economic field, by the Industrial Revolution and the strengthening of capitalism, which will have a response in Marxism and the class struggle.
qa_pairs.Q: What were the foundations of contemporary society laid in the 18th and 19th centuries?
qa_pairs.A: They were marked in the political field by the end of absolutism and the establishment of democratic governments, and in the economic field, by the Industrial Revolution and the strengthening of capitalism.

qa_pairs.S: The romantic nude is more expressive, more importance is given to color than to the line of the figure—unlike in neoclassicism—with a more dramatic sense, in themes that vary from the exotic and the taste for orientalism to the most purely romantic themes: dramas, tragedies, heroic and passionate acts, exacerbated feelings, songs to freedom, to the pure expression of the interior of the human being.
qa_pairs.Q: How does the romantic nude differ from the neoclassical nude?
qa_pairs.A: The romantic nude is more expressive, with more importance given to color than to the line of the figure, unlike in neoclassicism. It has a more dramatic sense, with themes that vary from the exotic and the taste for orientalism to the most purely romantic themes.

qa_pairs.S: In sculpture, Lorenzo Bartolini evolved from classicism to a naturalism inspired by the plastic models of the Florentine Quattrocento, as in Trust in God (1835).
qa_pairs.Q: What inspired Lorenzo Bartolini's naturalism?
qa_pairs.A: Plastic models of the Florentine Quattrocento.

qa_pairs.S: Impressionism was a profoundly innovative movement, which meant a break with academic art and a transformation of artistic language, initiating the path towards avant-garde movements.
qa_pairs.Q: What did Impressionism mean for the art world?
qa_pairs.A: A break with academic art and a transformation of artistic language, initiating the path towards avant-garde movements.

qa_pairs.S: Symbolism was a fantastic and dreamlike style, which emerged as a reaction to the naturalism of the realist and impressionist currents, placing special emphasis on the world of dreams, as well as on satanic and terrifying aspects, sex and perversion.
qa_pairs.Q: What was the main characteristic of Symbolism?
qa_pairs.A: Symbolism placed special emphasis on the world of dreams, as well as on satanic and terrifying aspects, sex and perversion.

qa_pairs.S: One of the characteristics of symbolism is the dark attraction to the perverse woman, the femme fatale, the Eve turned into Lilith, the enigmatic and distant, disturbing woman, the woman that Manuel Machado defined as 'brittle, vicious and mystical, pre-Raphaelite virgin and Parisian cat'.
qa_pairs.Q: What is the dark attraction in Symbolism?
qa_pairs.A: The dark attraction in Symbolism is to the perverse woman, the femme fatale, the Eve turned into Lilith, the enigmatic and distant, disturbing woman.

qa_pairs.S: Odilon Redon developed a work of strong oneiric content, finding in dreams an inexhaustible source of inspiration, with a style based on a soft drawing and a coloring of phosphorescent aspect (The Cyclops, 1898–1900).
qa_pairs.Q: What was the source of inspiration for Odilon Redon's work?
qa_pairs.A: In dreams.

qa_pairs.S: In the early years of the 20th century the foundations of the so-called avant-garde art were forged: the concept of reality was questioned by new scientific theories (Bergson's subjectivity of time, Einstein's relativity, quantum mechanics); Freud's theory of psychoanalysis also had an influence.
qa_pairs.Q: What were the factors that brought about a change of sensibility in the early 20th century art?
qa_pairs.A: New scientific theories, such as Bergson's subjectivity of time and Einstein's relativity, and Freud's theory of psychoanalysis brought about a change of sensibility in the early 20th century art.

qa_pairs.S: This movement was based on the deformation of reality through the destruction of the spatial perspective of Renaissance origin, organizing space according to a geometric grid, with simultaneous vision of objects, a range of cold and muted colors, and a new conception of the work of art, with the introduction of collage.
qa_pairs.Q: What was the main characteristic of Cubism?
qa_pairs.A: Deformation of reality through the destruction of the spatial perspective of Renaissance origin, organizing space according to a geometric grid, with simultaneous vision of objects, a range of cold and muted colors, and a new conception of the work of art, with the introduction of collage.

qa_pairs.S: Surrealism placed special emphasis on imagination, fantasy, the world of dreams, with a strong influence of psychoanalysis, as can be seen in its concept of 'automatic writing', by which they try to express themselves by freeing their minds from any rational bondage, to show the purity of the unconscious.
qa_pairs.Q: What was the main influence of psychoanalysis on Surrealism?
qa_pairs.A: The main influence of psychoanalysis on Surrealism was the emphasis on imagination, fantasy, the world of dreams, with a strong influence of psychoanalysis, as can be seen in its concept of 'automatic writing', by which they try to express themselves by freeing their minds from any rational bondage, to show the purity of the unconscious.

qa_pairs.S: In 1928, he settled in Paris, where he entered surrealism, of which he would be one of its main representatives, and the following year he met Gala Éluard, who would be his great muse, and whom he portrayed on numerous occasions, some of them nude.

qa_pairs.S: It emerged in Great Britain and the United States as a movement to reject abstract expressionism, encompassing a series of authors who returned to figuration, with a marked component of popular inspiration, taking images from the world of advertising, photography, comics and mass media. Pop-art assumed sex as something natural, unabashedly, within the framework of the sexual liberation of the 1960s advocated by the hippie movement.
qa_pairs.Q: What is pop-art in art?

qa_pairs.S: New Realism (1958–1970). French movement inspired by the world of the surrounding reality, consumerism and industrial society, from which they extract—unlike pop-art—its most unpleasant aspect, with a special predilection for detrimental materials.
qa_pairs.Q: What is New Realism?
qa_pairs.A: New Realism is a French movement inspired by the world of the surrounding reality, consumerism and industrial society, from which they extract its most unpleasant aspect, with a special predilection for detrimental materials.

qa_pairs.S: Hyperrealism (from 1965). As a reaction to the minimalism in vogue in the 1950s and 1960s, this new figurative current emerged, characterized by its superlative and exaggerated vision of reality, which is captured with great accuracy in all its details, with an almost photographic aspect.

qa_pairs.A: Hyperrealism is a new figurative current emerged from 1965 as a reaction to the minimalism in vogue in the 1950s and 1960s, characterized by its superlative and exaggerated vision of reality, which is captured with great accuracy in all its details, with an almost photographic aspect.

qa_pairs.S: The Gothic Cross. Erected in 1814 from Coade stone on the path linking the Doric Arch to the Temple of Ancient Virtue. It was erected by the 1st Duke of Buckingham and Chandos as a memorial to his mother Lady Mary Nugent. It was demolished in the 1980s by a falling elm tree. The National Trust rebuilt the cross in 2016 using several of the surviving pieces of the monument.
qa_pairs.Q: When was the Gothic Cross rebuilt by the National Trust?

qa_pairs.S: The Temple of Friendship. Built of stone in 1739 to the designs of Gibbs. It is located in the south-east corner of the garden. Inscribed on the exterior of the building is AMICITIAE S (sacred to friendship). It was badly damaged by fire in 1840 and remains a ruin.
qa_pairs.Q: What is inscribed on the exterior of the Temple of Friendship?
qa_pairs.A: AMICITIAE S (sacred to friendship).

qa_pairs.S: The Oxford Gates. The central piers were designed by William Kent in 1731, for a position to the north-east between the two Boycott Pavilions, they were moved to their present location in 1761, and iron railings added either side.

qa_pairs.S: Mannerism emerged in Italy as a natural evolution of the Renaissance, which had flourished between the 14th and 15th centuries, spreading a return to the classicist aesthetic ideals of formal balance, economy of means, and moderation in expressiveness, ideals that were associated with the highest moral values.

qa_pairs.S: Renowned Italian engineers and architects settled in our country, such as Benedict of Ravenna and Filippo Terzi, Giovanni Battista Antonelli and Giovanni Vincenzo Casale (and, later, Leonardo Turrano), contributed decisively to the full acceptance, in the Portuguese Empire, of a Mannerist architecture with a sui generis feture, curiously with a much more extensive chronological development than the other artistic branches, which already in the first third of the 17th century received the naturalistic influxes of the Baroque.
qa_pairs.Q: Who were the Italian engineers and architects that contributed to the Mannerist architecture in the Portuguese Empire?

qa_pairs.S: Among other things, it was responsible for the supply of food and water, the maintenance of roads, the census of the population and territorial demarcation.
qa_pairs.Q: What was the Council of One Hundred responsible for during medieval times in Barcelona?
qa_pairs.A: The supply of food and water, the maintenance of roads, the census of the population and territorial demarcation.

qa_pairs.S: Located on a small peninsula of land reclaimed from the sea, its layout was designed by the engineer Pedro Martín Cermeño, with a grid of orthogonal streets and blocks of houses of elongated plan, which is a clear example of academic baroque urbanism.
qa_pairs.Q: Who designed the layout of the neighborhood of La Barceloneta?
qa_pairs.A: The engineer Pedro Martín Cermeño.

qa_pairs.S: The population grew, especially thanks to immigration from the rest of Spain, reaching 400,000 inhabitants by the end of the century.
qa_pairs.Q: What was the main reason for the population growth in Barcelona during the 19th century?
qa_pairs.A: Due to immigration from the rest of Spain.

qa_pairs.S: In 1859 the City Council appointed a commission to promote a competition for urban expansion projects, which was won by Antoni Rovira i Trias; however, the Ministry of Development intervened and imposed the project of Ildefons Cerdà, author of a topographic plan of the Barcelona plain and a demographic and urbanistic study of the city (1855).
qa_pairs.Q: Who won the competition for urban expansion projects in Barcelona in 1859?
qa_pairs.A: Antoni Rovira i Trias.

qa_pairs.S: The buildings were to have a height of 16 meters (first floor and four floors), and a depth of 10 to 20 meters. The distribution of the Eixample was to be in sectors of 20 x 20 blocks, divided into districts of 10 x 10 and neighborhoods of 5 x 5.
qa_pairs.Q: What was the planned height of the buildings in Eixample?
qa_pairs.A: 16 meters.

qa_pairs.S: The naming of the new streets was entrusted to the writer Víctor Balaguer, who was inspired by the history of Catalonia: Thus, many streets are named after territories linked to the Crown of Aragon, such as Valencia, Mallorca, Aragon, Provence, Roussillon, Naples, Corsica, Sicily or Sardinia; with institutions such as the Catalan Courts, the Generalitat or the Consell de Cent; with characters such as Jaime Balmes, Enrique Granados, Buenaventura Carlos Aribau, Ramón Muntaner, Rafael Casanova, Pau Claris, Roger de Flor, Antoni de Villarroel, Roger de Lauria, Ausiàs March or the Count of Urgel; or battles and historical events such as Bailén, Lepanto, El Bruch or Caspe.
qa_pairs.Q: Who was responsible for naming the new streets in Eixample?
qa_pairs.A: Víctor Balaguer.

qa_pairs.S: On the other hand, the increase in population between the nineteenth and twentieth centuries led to the creation of new hospitals to serve the population of the new districts of the city: the Hospital Clínico y Provincial (1895-1906) and the Hospital de la Santa Creu i Sant Pau (1902-1930), a monumental modernist-style complex designed by Lluís Domènech i Montaner.
qa_pairs.Q: What hospitals were created to serve the population of the new districts of the city?
qa_pairs.A: The Hospital Clínico y Provincial, the Hospital de la Santa Creu i Sant Pau, the hospitals of Esperança, the Red Cross, the Military, and the Hospital del Mar were created to serve the population of the new districts of the city. (from: [seg])

qa_pairs.S: The Jaussely Plan was based on a structural scheme, with a differentiated treatment of the various urban fabrics, which recalls the Beaux-Arts type layouts in vogue in the international environments of the time.

qa_pairs.S: In 1929 the International Exposition was held in Montjuïc. For this event the entire area of the Plaça dEspanya, the avenue of Queen Maria Christina and the mountain of Montjuïc was urbanized, and the pavilions that currently house the Barcelona Fair were built.
qa_pairs.Q: What was the purpose of the International Exposition held in Montjuïc in 1929?
qa_pairs.A: To urbanize the entire area of the Plaça dEspanya, the avenue of Queen Maria Christina and the mountain of Montjuïc and build pavilions that currently house the Barcelona Fair.

qa_pairs.S: The construction of housing was carried out, in many cases, without prior urban planning, and using cheap materials that, over the years, would cause various problems such as aluminosis.
qa_pairs.Q: What were the consequences of the construction of housing during the Franco dictatorship?
qa_pairs.A: It was carried out without prior urban planning and using cheap materials that caused various problems such as aluminosis.

qa_pairs.S: The post-war urban renewal was led by the head of urban planning of the new authorities, Pedro Bidagor, who in 1945 promoted the creation of the Barcelona Provincial Planning Commission, responsible for drawing up a planning project for the city and its surroundings.
qa_pairs.Q: What's the creation of the Barcelona Provincial Planning Commission responsible for ?
qa_pairs.A: Drawing up a planning project for the city and its surroundings.

qa_pairs.S: Thus arose the Regional Plan of 1953, developed by Josep Soteras, an attempt to integrate the city with neighboring municipalities in order to meet the strong demand for housing in the years of massive immigration, while trying to curb real estate speculation and improve the urban environment.
qa_pairs.Q: What was the Regional Plan of 1953?
qa_pairs.A: An attempt to integrate the city with neighboring municipalities in order to meet the strong demand for housing in the years of massive immigration, while trying to curb real estate speculation and improve the urban environment.

qa_pairs.S: Between 1983 and 1989 the concept of 'areas of new centrality' was developed, in search of a more polycentric and better connected city.
qa_pairs.Q: What was the aim of the concept of 'areas of new centrality'?
qa_pairs.A: The aim was to decongest the center by promoting various sectors of the urban periphery, which should regenerate low-quality urban fabrics thanks to their intrinsic morphological qualities.

qa_pairs.S: The site was designed by Elías Torres and José Antonio Martínez Lapeña, with a 16-hectare multipurpose esplanade culminating at one end with a large photovoltaic panel, which became one of the emblems of the event.

The answer is:  Naach, also known as Launda Naach, is a dance form originating in the state of Bihar and practiced in neighboring states like Uttar Pradesh. It was a popular folk dance of the depressed castes and its themes included the "caste struggle" inherent in the society of Bihar. Over time, the dance was associated with the dominant castes, and Bhikhari Thakur reorganized it to rename it as Bideshiya. The term Launda refers to a person who is effeminate, vulgar, immature, and from an inferior class or caste. The dance form's performances often attacked the upper castes for the atrocities they committed on the lower castes, leading to its stigmatization by upper castes as vulgar and uncouth. One of the popular performances was the play Ghurva Chamaar, which portrayed the challenges faced by Dalits in accessing religious spaces.

qa_pairs.S: The four elders want extravagant garments and shoes, but the youngest asks for a clustered bunch of grape-like pearls and a starred, two-pointed diamond.
qa_pairs.Q: What does Yasmin ask for as a gift from her father?
qa_pairs.A: A clustered bunch of grape-like pearls and a starred, two-pointed diamond.

qa_pairs.S: In his Catalogue of Persian Folktales, German scholar Ulrich Marzolph [de] classified the tale as his type AaTh 425B, Der Tierbräutigam: Die böse Zauberin ('The Animal Bridegroom: The Evil Sorceress').
qa_pairs.Q: What is the classification of Yasmin and the Serpent Prince folktale?
qa_pairs.A: Type AaTh 425B.

qa_pairs.S: Type 425B is considered by scholarship to correspond to the ancient Graeco-Roman myth of Cupid and Psyche, that is, the supernatural husband's mother forces the heroine, her daughter-in-law, to perform difficult and impossible tasks for her.
qa_pairs.Q: What is the mythological origin of type 425B?
qa_pairs.A: Ancient Graeco-Roman myth of Cupid and Psyche.

qa_pairs.S: One of the heroine's tasks is to sort out a heap of mixed grains. According to Swedish scholar Jan-Öjvind Swahn [sv], this motif appears in Mediterranean and Near Eastern variants of type ATU 425B, 'The Witch's Tasks'.
qa_pairs.Q: What is the significance of sorting out a heap of mixed grains in type 425B?
qa_pairs.A: One of the heroine's tasks in type 425B and is a motif that appears in Mediterranean and Near Eastern variants of the tale.

qa_pairs.S: The girl goes to ask her husband the question. In a fit of fury, he slaps her so hard she passes out. When she wakes up, Malik Ibrahim answers her: burn the dragonskin in onion peels and garlic peels, with a sprinkle of salt.
qa_pairs.Q: What is the solution to destroy the dragonskin?
qa_pairs.A: The dragonskin can be destroyed by burning it in onion peels and garlic peels, with a sprinkle of salt.

qa_pairs.S: In an Iranian tale published by author Moniro Ravanipour with the title 'سبزقبا' ('Sabzqaba'), a couple has no children. One day, the wife goes to the spring to fetch water and sees a tree with a green trunk, which she promises to deliver her daughter to if one is born to her.
qa_pairs.Q: What does the woman promise to the tree?
qa_pairs.A: Deliver her daughter to the tree if one is born to her.

qa_pairs.S: In a variant from the Lezgin people titled 'Сад-Эскендер' ('Sad-Eskender'), collected in Dagestan, a poor man lives with his three daughters, Gul-Khanum, Guzel-Khanum and Tavat-Khanum. One day, he goes to the open fields and says his prayers there, when a snake slithers from beneath a stone and asks to marry one of the man's daughters.
qa_pairs.Q: What does the snake ask the man's daughters?
qa_pairs.A: The snake asks to marry one of the man's daughters.

qa_pairs.S: The girl asks for some water, the youngest woman gives her a jug to drink from and Tavat-Khanum drops her ring in it.
qa_pairs.Q: What does Tavat-Khanum drop in the jug?
qa_pairs.A: Her ring.

qa_pairs.S: The snake demands one of the man's daughters in marriage, otherwise it will destroy the fields.
qa_pairs.Q: What does the snake threaten to do if it doesn't get one of the man's daughters?
qa_pairs.A: The snake threatens to destroy the fields if it doesn't get one of the man's daughters.

qa_pairs.S: Turkish folklorist Saim Sakaoğlu [tr] collected the tale Sır Saklamayan Padişah Kızı ('The Padishah's Daughter Who Cannot Keep a Secret') from the region of Gümüşhane, which was translated Into German by Adelheid Uzunoğlu-Ocherbauer as Die Prinzessin, die kein Geheimnis für sich behalten konnte ('The Princess who could not Keep a Secret').
qa_pairs.Q: Who collected the tale Sır Saklamayan Padişah Kızı?
qa_pairs.A: Turkish folklorist Saim Sakaoğlu.

qa_pairs.S: In an Azeri tale titled 'Шамси-Камар' ('Shamsi-Kamar' or 'Sun-Moon'),[38] by analysing the ripeness of three melons, the king notices it is past time to marry his three daughters, so he organizes a contest: his daughters should cast three arrows at random, see where they land and marry the man that lives wherever the arrows land on.
qa_pairs.Q: What does the king do in the Azeri tale 'Шамси-Камар'?
qa_pairs.A: He organizes a contest.

qa_pairs.S: Shakarkhava, in despair, sees her husband disappear, so she commissions seven pairs of iron shoes, seven iron dresses and seven iron canes, and goes on a quest for him.
qa_pairs.Q: What does Shakarkhava do after her husband disappears?
qa_pairs.A: Shakarkhava commissions iron shoes, dresses, and canes and goes on a quest for her husband.

qa_pairs.S: Shafi Guli Zard and the false bride enter the bridal chambers. At midnight, he puts some cotton on the wedding bells to muffle them, goes to Fatma-Pleshak (his true wife) and they escape in the dark of night.
qa_pairs.Q: Who does Shafi Guli Zard escape with?
qa_pairs.A: Fatma-Pleshak.

qa_pairs.S: Bibi Sarvar tries to burn the snakeskin at first, but it resists the flames. The girl asks her husband, Shams-e-Qamar, about the correct way to do it; he tells her it can be burnt with garlic and onion, but warns her against it.
qa_pairs.Q: What does Shams-e-Qamar warn Bibi Sarvar about?
qa_pairs.A: He warns her against burning the snakeskin with garlic and onion.

qa_pairs.S: The shepherd tells the girl's mother about it, and she decides to fulfill her deal by delivering her daughter to the tree.
qa_pairs.Q: What does the girl's mother decide to do?
qa_pairs.A: Deliver her daughter to the tree.

qa_pairs.S: The giantess tries to cross the sea, but she drowns.

qa_pairs.S: The pair returns safely to Yemen, and restores the Yemeni royal couple's health.

qa_pairs.S: The poem contains Iqbal’s inventive doctrine of the self; and all the subsequent works of Iqbal supplemented and further refined this central concept.

qa_pairs.S: Iqbal emphasizes the paramount place of the physical world in an effort to realize the spiritual ideal of growth of the selfhood.

qa_pairs.S: To Iqbal Khudi is a fact and not an illusion. It is neither an abstract thought nor an idea. He says, 'If you say that 'I' is a mere imagination; And its appearance is mere appearance; Then tell me, who is it that entertains these imagination; Just look within and think what this appearanceis.'
qa_pairs.Q: What does Iqbal believe about Khudi?
qa_pairs.A: Iqbal believes that Khudi is a fact and not an illusion. It is neither an abstract thought nor an idea.

qa_pairs.S: To Iqbal, the criterion of the degree of reality of any living organism is the extent to which it has achieved the feeling of a distinct Khudi: He says, 'Only that truly exist which can say 'I am'.

qa_pairs.A: The extent to which it has achieved the feeling of a distinct Khudi.

qa_pairs.S: Iqbal lays great emphasis on the value of love for strengthening Khudi. To him love for an individual means the assimilation and absorption of the characteristics prominent in the beloved.

llm_output: The answer is:  The central concept of Iqbal's poetry is the concept of Khudi, which emphasizes the importance of self-realization, self-affirmation, and the development of individuality.

The answer is:  In Iqbal's concept of selfhood, the physical world plays a paramount place as it is essential for the realization of the spiritual ideal of growth of selfhood. Iqbal emphasizes that the self cannot develop in a vacuum and that the society plays a dominant role in developing human personality. The physical world serves as a training ground for intellectual vision and helps man maintain his life in an obstructing environment. Thus, the physical world is indispensable for the growth and realization of the self.

The answer is:  In Iqbal's poetry, the burning passion of desire is significant as it leads to the awakening of the self. This awakening is characterized by the burning of the passion of desire and the creation of ideals. Purpose and desire are seminal for the growth of the self towards actualization and realization. Desire is the spring from which the self draws sustenance, and it is essential for a life fraught with meaning, purpose, and ideal. Without it, life is described as dreariness, decay, and finally death. Desire, in Iqbal's view, is the driving force that propels the self towards its realization, making it a crucial element in the process of self-development and self-actualization.

The answer is:  Iqbal believes that Khudi (self or ego) is a universal reality that is the center and basis of the entire organization of life. It is a fact, not an illusion, and its reality is undeniable. Khudi is not independent but dependent on God, the infinite ego, for its existence. It has the potential for growth and corruption, and its individuality diminishes as it moves further from God. Iqbal emphasizes that the development of Khudi is crucial for the growth of the self, and it can achieve its highest stage through Divine Vicegerency, where it can absorb God's attributes and become a Perfect Man, maintaining its individuality even in the presence of the Infinite Khudi.
The answer is:  The criterion of the degree of reality of any living organism is the extent to which it has achieved the feeling of a distinct Khudi (self).

The answer is:  To Iqbal, love is a desire to assimilate and absorb, and its highest form is the creation of values and ideals and the endeavor to realize them. Love individualizes both the lover and the beloved, and it is a force that can lead to the absorption of the Divine attributes within the self, thereby strengthening the Khudi.

The answer is:  According to Iqbal, the cultivation of an attitude of courage is essential for the proper education of character. Courage, as a positive force, strengthens the Khudi (self), releases its potential capacity for great needs, and contributes to the development of creativity and originality. On the other hand, fear, which is the negation of courage, weakens the Khudi and becomes the source of all kinds of corruption in the individual character.
The answer is:  Obedience to law and self-control act as milestones on the upward march towards the goal of Naab in the development of Khudi.

The answer is:  According to Iqbal, the psychological difference between the prophetic and the mystic types of consciousness lies in the mystic's desire to remain in the repose of 'unitary experience,' even when they return to the material world, which does not significantly impact mankind at large. In contrast, the prophet returns from this experience to actively engage with the world, controlling historical forces and creating a fresh world of ideals, thereby contributing meaningfully to humanity.

The answer is:  According to Iqbal, man molds his destiny through constant movement and effort. By overcoming the tension between the "creator man" and the "Creator God," and by striving to achieve endless perfection through the pull of love and desire, man directs the universe toward ever-increasing perfection. This striving ensures that society's wellbeing is ensured and maintained, and it is through this inner, creative tension that man continually recreates himself and his society, ultimately shedding the shackles of colonialism.

The answer is:  Through striving to do good deeds, the soul attains the highest level by training itself to become in tune with the divine, ultimately allowing one to achieve a state of peace and harmony with God.
The answer is:  The notions of "autonomy, as choosing our ends, and the means towards them" are built into the modern, Western notion of freedom.

The answer is:  Nationalist thought operates within a framework of knowledge whose representational structure corresponds to the very structure of power it seeks to repudiate. This means that while nationalism aims to liberate the nation from colonialism, it does so using knowledge systems dominated by a post-Enlightenment West, which continue to exert a form of power and dominance.
The answer is:  According to Iqbal, the self gets nourishment through desires and the formation of new purposes and objects.
The answer is:  Iqbal believes that the cultivation of an attitude of courage is essential for the proper education of character.

qa_pairs.S: The DCPCR recommended the following guidelines to ensure the well-being of children in same-sex adoptions: The Union and State Governments create public awareness that same-sex families are as 'normal' as opposite-sex families and that children of same-sex families are not incomplete.[26] School boards and educational institutions normalise same-sex families proactively.[26] National and State Council for Education, Research and Training to review and eliminate homophobic content and include a more diverse understanding of the families in school textbooks.[26] Relevant Authorities should create dedicated helplines and set aside resources for counselling for children facing stigma and bullying due to belonging to a same-sex family unit.

qa_pairs.Q: What caused the massive traffic tie-ups in Akron?
qa_pairs.A: Icy pavement and blowing snow.

qa_pairs.S: In Washington D.C., gales of 78 mph (126 km/h) blew parts of the roofs of two apartment buildings off, downed power lines down and uprooted trees, including one which demolished a car being driven on the Rock Creek Parkway—the driver only suffered minor injuries.

qa_pairs.A: The driver only suffered minor injuries.

qa_pairs.Q: How many fatalities were caused by the blizzard in west-central New York?
qa_pairs.A: Seven.

qa_pairs.S: In 1155, Henry expelled foreign mercenaries and ordered the demolition of illegal castles.
qa_pairs.Q: What did Henry do in 1155 to restore royal authority?
qa_pairs.A: Henry expelled foreign mercenaries and ordered the demolition of illegal castles.

qa_pairs.S: In 1258, the king was forced to submit to a radical reform programme promulgated at the Oxford Parliament. The Provisions of Oxford transferred royal power to a council of fifteen barons.
qa_pairs.Q: What was the reform programme promulgated at the Oxford Parliament?
qa_pairs.A: The Provisions of Oxford.

qa_pairs.S: Previous monarchs were only legally recognised as king after coronation, but Edward's reign officially began on 20 November, the same day his father was buried at Westminster Abbey. Walter Giffard, archbishop of York; Roger Mortimer, a marcher lord; and Robert Burnell were appointed regents.
qa_pairs.Q: When did Edward I's reign officially begin?
qa_pairs.A: 20 November.

qa_pairs.S: His first act was ordering the Hundred Rolls survey, a detailed investigation into what rights and land the Crown had lost since Henry III's reign.
qa_pairs.Q: What was the purpose of the Hundred Rolls survey ordered by Edward I?
qa_pairs.A: Investigate what rights and land the Crown had lost since Henry III's reign and to root out corruption by royal officials.

qa_pairs.S: Richard II was not the first English monarch to be deposed; that distinction belongs to Edward II. Edward abdicated in favor of his son and heir.
qa_pairs.Q: Who was the first English monarch to be deposed?

qa_pairs.S: Henry VII then neutralised the remaining Yorkist forces, partly by marrying Elizabeth of York, a Yorkist heir.
qa_pairs.Q: How did Henry VII neutralize the remaining Yorkist forces?
qa_pairs.A: By marrying Elizabeth of York, a Yorkist heir.

qa_pairs.Q: When were the English and Scottish kingdoms ruled by a single sovereign?
qa_pairs.A: From 1603,.

qa_pairs.A: The evolution of the Dominions of the Empire into separate, self-governing countries within a Commonwealth of Nations.

He had spent most of his life in Normandy and culturally was 'probably more French than English'.
qa_pairs.Q: Who was Edward the Confessor's parents?
qa_pairs.A: Edward the Confessor was the son of Æthelred and Emma.

qa_pairs.S: Symbolism emerged as a reaction to the multiple tendencies linked to realism in the field of culture throughout the 19th century. Factors such as the progress of science since the Renaissance.
qa_pairs.Q: What was the main reason for the emergence of Symbolism?
qa_pairs.A: A reaction to the multiple tendencies linked to realism in the field of culture throughout the 19th century.

qa_pairs.S: In painting, symbolism was a fantastic and dreamlike style that emerged as a reaction to the naturalism of the realist painting and impressionist trends, whose objectivity and detailed description of reality were opposed by subjectivity and the depiction of the occult and the irrational, as opposed to representation, evocation, or suggestion.
qa_pairs.Q: What was the main difference between Symbolist painting and realist painting?
qa_pairs.A: Symbolist painting was a fantastic and dreamlike style that emerged as a reaction to the naturalism of the realist painting and impressionist trends.

qa_pairs.S: Symbolism was spread by numerous magazines such as La Revue wagnerienne (1885), Le Symbolisme (1886), La Plume (1889), La Revue blanche (1891) and, especially, La Pléiade (1886, renamed in 1889 as Mercure de France), which was the official organ of symbolism.
qa_pairs.Q: What were some of the magazines that spread Symbolism?
qa_pairs.A: La Revue wagnerienne, Le Symbolisme, La Plume, La Revue blanche, and La Pléiade.

qa_pairs.S: Decadentism was an anti-bourgeois and anti-naturalist movement, which defended luxury, pleasure and hypersensitivity of taste.

qa_pairs.S: Symbolism influenced several contemporary movements, such as modernism and naïve art, as well as several of the early 'isms' of avant-garde art, such as fauvism, expressionism, futurism, surrealism and even abstract art

qa_pairs.S: In a tale published by Chinese author Lin Lan and translated by Juwen Zhang as The Snake Spirit, an old man has three daughters. One day, he goes to cut wood in the mountains and picks some flowers on their request. Suddenly, a snake spirit appears as a young human youth and asks the reason why he is fetching the flowers.
qa_pairs.Q: What does the snake spirit ask the old man?
qa_pairs.A: The snake spirit asks the reason why he is fetching the flowers.

qa_pairs.S: In another tale published by Lin Lan and translated by Juwen Zhang as The Garden Snake, a man goes to the mountains to chop wood and falls into a trap set by a garden snake with its skin. The man pleads for his life in name of his three daughters, which piques the snake's interest in marrying one of the man's daughters.
qa_pairs.Q: What piques the garden snake's interest in marrying one of the man's daughters?
qa_pairs.A: The man pleads for his life in name of his three daughters, which piques the snake's interest in marrying one of the man's daughters.

qa_pairs.S: The snake ponders how the man can pay him back, and asks if he has any daughters, and offer a proposition: Jihong will have to surrender one of his daughters to the snake as its bride. Jihong returns home and explains the situation to his daughters, but only the youngest decides to take up on the snake's deal.
qa_pairs.Q: Why did the man agree to marry one of his daughters to the white serpent prince?
qa_pairs.A: The animal explains he is the white serpent prince of the mountains, and asks for one of the man's daughters in marriage in exchange for returning the ax.

qa_pairs.S: In a Vietnamese tale attributed to the Meo people, 'Юноша в образе змеи' ('The Youth in the Form of a Snake'), a widowed father has three daughters, the youngest the most beautiful and industrious, the elder two idle and arrogant.
qa_pairs.Q: What does the youngest daughter do when the snake comes to their house?
qa_pairs.A: The youngest daughter invites the snake in, cooks some rice for it, and prepares a bed for it as if it is a normal guest.

qa_pairs.S: In a Nepalese tale collected in Dsarkot, Mustang with the title Der Hundebräutigam ('The Hound Bridegroom'), a woman has three unmarried daughters, which saddens her. So, she plays a ruse on them: she pretends to be on her deathbed and asks for her daughters to bring her some grass and water from a remote valley.
qa_pairs.Q: What does the woman ask her daughters to bring her?
qa_pairs.A: Some grass and water from a remote valley.

qa_pairs.S: The false wife orders the plants to be made into firewood; a poor couple fetches some, bring it with them and place it in a box.
qa_pairs.Q: Who brings the firewood to the false wife?
qa_pairs.A: A poor couple.

qa_pairs.S: The man returns home and tells the situation to his daughters. The three elders refuse to marry the snake, despite his threat, but the youngest, Almond Blossom, being the 'most devotedly filial', offers to go in her father's place. The fairies listen to her plea and, touched by her devotion, send one of their own to protect her against her enemies.
qa_pairs.Q: Who offers to go in her father's place as the snake's bride?
qa_pairs.A: The youngest daughter.

qa_pairs.S: In the early 20th century, Pieter Geyl brought a new perspective on the Revolt by arguing that Belgian and Dutch historians such as Henri Pirenne had been led astray by hindsight bias: they assumed that the eventual modern state borders between Belgium and the Netherlands were the result of the logical course of history, whereas it made more sense to Geyl if the state borders had coincided with the Franco–Dutch language border.

qa_pairs.S: Models of the interplanetary dust cloud require that the lifetimes of interplanetary dust particles are longer than those for rock material and, hence, support the result that at 1 AU ~80% of the interplanetary dust is of cometary origin and only ~20% of asteroidal origin.

qa_pairs.S: Collisional fragmentation leads to a net loss of interplanetary dust particles more massive than ~2×10−9 kg and a net gain of less massive interplanetary dust particles. Comets are believed to replenish the losses of big interplanetary dust.

qa_pairs.S: Early infrared observations of the solar corona during an eclipse indicated a dust-free zone inside ~5 solar radii (0.025 AU) from the sun. Outside of this dust-free zone interplanetary dust consisting of silicates and cacarbonaceous material will sublimate at temperatures up to 2000 K.

qa_pairs.S: The sputter yield denotes the average number of atoms expelled from the target per incident atom or ion. The sputter yield primarily relies on the energy and mass of the incident particles, as well as the mass of the target atoms.

The answer is:  After 1855, the situation for the Choctaw who remained in or returned to Mississippi deteriorated. Many lost their lands and money to unscrupulous whites. The state of Mississippi refused the Choctaw any participation in government. Most were isolated by their limited understanding of the English language, which made it difficult for them to work in mainstream society. Additionally, they were classified as free people of color and excluded from segregated white educational institutions. The state had no public schools before those established during the Reconstruction era, leaving the Choctaw at the mercy of whites who could commit crimes against them without fear of the law. Even black slaves had more legal rights than the Choctaw during this period.

qa_pairs.S: Thomas Barrett – 27 February 1788 – Barrett was publicly hanged at Sydney Cove for stealing or conspiring to steal from government stores. He was the first person hanged in the colony of New South Wales.

qa_pairs.S: John Brown – 30 May 1808 – Hanged at Sydney. A convict who escaped from custody and remained at large in the Van Diemen's Land wilderness for some 20 months. During this time, with John Lemon (Lemon was shot dead while resisting capture) he was involved in the murder of three soldiers, Corporal John Curry, Private Robert Grindstone and Private James Daniels. For his involvement in the crimes Brown was transported from Van Diemen's Land to Sydney to stand trial. His body was dissected and gibbeted.
qa_pairs.Q: What was John Brown's punishment for his involvement in the murder of three soldiers?
qa_pairs.A: He was transported to Sydney to stand trial. His body was dissected and gibbeted

qa_pairs.S: By February 1304 all the leading Scots, except for William Wallace, surrendered to Edward I. qa_pairs.A: All the leading Scots, except for William Wallace.

qa_pairs.S: Robert the Bruce, former Guardian of Scotland in 1305 was accused of treason by Edward I and returned to Scotland. On 25 March 1306 he was crowned Robert I, King of Scots witnessed by the bishops of Moray and as such the new ruler of Nairn.
qa_pairs.Q: Who accused Robert the Bruce of treason?
qa_pairs.A: Edward I.

qa_pairs.S: Alexander ruled these territories with the help of his own private cateran forces, building up resentment among other land owners and this included Alexander Bur, Bishop of Moray.

qa_pairs.S: Former Prime Minister Paul Keating said: 'She was an exemplar of public leadership, married for a lifetime to political restraint, remaining always, the constitutional monarch... qa_pairs.Q: What was the reason for the lack of national over-the-air network coverage of the NHL in the United States?
qa_pairs.A: No network was willing to commit to carrying a large number of games, in turn providing low ratings for NHL telecasts.

qa_pairs.S: ABC would eventually resume the network broadcasting of regular NHL games (on a time buy basis through ESPN) for the 1992–93 season.

qa_pairs.S: Beginning with the 2008–09 season, Hockey Nights main games were simulcast weekly in the United States on NHL Network, complete with pre- and post-game shows.

qa_pairs.S: ESPN will hold rights to 25 exclusive national games per season, which can air on either ESPN or ABC, and will include exclusive rights to opening night games.
